name: Movie Library (Refactor to Objects)
scenario:
You're building the backend for a movie review platform (think IMDb, BookMyShow, or a personal collection tracker). The current code uses parallel arrays to track movie data — title, director, year, rating. It works, but it's getting harder to maintain. Adding fields like 'genre' or 'duration' would require updating multiple arrays. Sorting movies by rating would require keeping all arrays in sync. Time to refactor using OBJECTS.

This project is the FIRST experience of OOP improving real code. You'll start with parallel arrays (provided), refactor to a Movie class, and see your code become shorter, clearer, and easier to extend.
description: Build a Movie collection app that uses a `Movie` class instead of parallel arrays. Create movie objects, add them to an array, then implement queries: list all, find highest rated, count movies by a director, find longest-running.

core.description: Movie has fields: title (String), director (String), year (int), rating (double)
Movie has instance method `describe()` returning formatted string like 'Inception (2010) by Nolan - 8.8/10'
Main class `MovieLibrary` has a Movie[] field with at least 6 movies populated
`printAll()` method displays all movies using describe()
`findHighestRated(Movie[] movies)` method returns the Movie object with highest rating
`countByDirector(Movie[] movies, String director)` returns count of movies by that director
main() demonstrates all functionality with clear output

bonus.description: Add `findByYear(Movie[] movies, int year)` returning a Movie[] of movies released in that year (could be 0, 1, or many)
Add `isClassic()` instance method to Movie — returns true if year < 2000 AND rating >= 8.0
Add `printClassics(Movie[] movies)` that prints only classic movies

example_output:
===================================
        MOVIE LIBRARY
===================================
Inception (2010) by Nolan - 8.8/10
The Dark Knight (2008) by Nolan - 9.0/10
Pulp Fiction (1994) by Tarantino - 8.9/10
Interstellar (2014) by Nolan - 8.6/10
Kill Bill (2003) by Tarantino - 8.1/10
3 Idiots (2009) by Hirani - 8.4/10
-----------------------------------
Highest rated: The Dark Knight (2008) by Nolan - 9.0/10
Nolan movies: 3
Tarantino movies: 2
Hirani movies: 1
-----------------------------------
(Bonus) Average rating: 8.63
(Bonus) Classics:
  Pulp Fiction (1994) by Tarantino - 8.9/10
===================================

criteria: describe() is an instance method, not static (10 pts)
printAll iterates and uses describe (10 pts)
Output is organized and clear (5 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 AI hints. The key concepts (defining a class, creating objects, instance methods) are now familiar. The tricky parts are: (1) using .equals() for the director string comparison — NOT == (callback to Module 3!), and (2) findByYear returning a NEW array of variable size.
submission_instructions: Save as MovieLibrary.java. Run it. After working, look at your code and reflect: how much cleaner is this than the parallel-arrays approach? Push to GitHub — your first real OOP project.
tips_for_success: Movie class goes OUTSIDE the public MovieLibrary class (same file, NO `public` keyword for Movie)
Each Movie field is just declared (String title, etc.) — no value yet
describe() uses field names directly — no 'this.' needed, no parameters
For countByDirector: USE .equals() to compare director Strings, NEVER ==
findHighestRated: track the WHOLE Movie object (Movie max), not just the rating
For findByYear (bonus): make two passes — first count matches, then create array of right size and fill it
Or use a fixed-size 'output' array of length = input.length and a counter, then Arrays.copyOf(output, count)
concepts_reinforced: Class definition with fields and methods
Object arrays — must new each slot
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this:
This project is your FIRST OOP refactor. The Movie class with describe() is doing real work — bundling movie data and self-describing behavior. The methods findHighestRated and countByDirector operate on Movie objects, not separate arrays of fields.

Compare to the equivalent parallel-arrays code: you'd need separate arrays for title, director, year, rating, all kept in sync. To find Nolan movies, you'd loop through directors array and use indices to look up other fields. With Movies as objects, the loop is one line cleaner per access.

You'll especially feel the difference when you do the bonus: findByYear returns a Movie[]. With parallel arrays, you'd need to return THREE arrays (titles, directors, ratings) of matching size. With objects: one array of Movies. This is the OOP payoff.
extensions_for_advanced_learners: Add a runtime field (in minutes) and a 'isLongMovie' method (> 150 min)
Sort movies by rating descending (use bubble sort or Arrays.sort with Comparator — Comparator preview)
Group movies by decade and print each decade's count
Add a 'addReview(String reviewText, int score)' method that stores reviews (needs ArrayList — preview of Collections)
Refactor to use constructors when Topic 4.2 lands (preview)
